topic: Religion, Philosophy & Ethics: The Existence of God
subject: religious-studies

items.q: What does the cosmological argument actually claim?
items.correct: That everything that exists has a cause, an infinite regress of causes is impossible, so there must be an uncaused First Cause, which Aquinas identified as God.
items.misconception: The cosmological argument says God exists because the Bible says so.
items.random_wrong: The cosmological argument claims the universe is itself God.
items.why: Students confuse philosophical arguments with appeals to scripture; the cosmological argument deliberately reasons from observation of cause and effect, not from religious authority.

items.q: Does Paley's design argument prove God's existence with certainty?
items.correct: No — it is an inductive argument claiming design is the best explanation of order in nature; critics like Hume, and Darwin's evolution, offer alternative explanations, so it is probable at best.
items.misconception: Paley's watch analogy gives conclusive proof that God exists.
items.random_wrong: Paley argued that watches occur naturally without any maker.
items.why: Students overstate inductive arguments as proofs; evaluation marks require recognising the argument's probabilistic nature and its standard criticisms.

items.q: Is an agnostic the same as an atheist?
items.correct: No — an atheist believes there is no God, while an agnostic holds that God's existence cannot be known or proven either way.
items.misconception: Agnostics and atheists both assert that God definitely does not exist.
items.random_wrong: An agnostic is someone who attends church without enjoying it.
items.why: Students collapse the two positions, but the distinction between denial of God and suspension of judgement is a frequent definition question.

items.q: Paley's watchmaker analogy supports which argument for God's existence?
items.why: It's The design (teleological) argument. “The cosmological argument” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Aquinas's First Cause argument is a version of which argument?
items.why: It's The cosmological argument. “The design argument” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The problem of evil challenges which two attributes of God?
items.why: It's Omnipotence and omnibenevolence. “Transcendence and immanence” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Who defined God as 'that than which nothing greater can be conceived'?
items.why: Correct answer: Anselm. “Paley” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Paley publish Natural Theology, containing the watchmaker analogy?
items.why: Exact answer: 1802 year (CE). Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
